questions.question: Why are wall-clock timestamps risky as the only ordering signal?
options: Because different machines can drift and messages can arrive out of order, so timestamp order may not match real causal or business order.
Because timestamps cannot be stored in databases.
Because networked systems never use clocks at all.
Because display time and correctness time are always identical.
questions.explanation: Clock skew and delivery delay can make wall time misleading for correctness.

questions.question: What should you ask before relying on TTL or lease expiry?
options: How much skew is possible and what safety margin or fencing mechanism protects ownership decisions?
Whether the timestamp field fits in 64 bits.
How to remove version metadata entirely.
Whether caches can hide lease bugs.
questions.explanation: Time-based ownership needs explicit skew and fencing thinking.

questions.question: What is the hidden risk of last-write-wins under skewed clocks?
options: A write with an apparently newer timestamp can overwrite the more correct or later-intended state.
It forces every write through a global lock instantly.
It prevents replica repair completely.
It guarantees causal ordering.
questions.explanation: Timestamp freshness is not always the same as logical correctness.

questions.question: Why do fencing tokens help with lease ownership?
options: They let the system reject stale owners even if lease timing and delayed messages would otherwise create overlap.
They synchronize every node's wall clock perfectly.
They eliminate the need for monitoring.
They make retries unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Fencing protects against stale actors after ownership changes.

questions.question: Which metric most directly exposes dangerous skew for control paths?
options: Clock offset or fence-violation metrics by node or region.
The number of lesson exercises completed.
Average documentation line length.
How many worker binaries were built last week.
questions.explanation: Those metrics show whether timing assumptions are drifting toward unsafe behavior.

questions.question: When is logical versioning usually better than timestamps alone?
options: When correctness depends on ordering and conflicting writers or delayed delivery can make wall-clock order ambiguous.
Only when there is a single writer on one machine.
Only in batch analytics systems.
When user-visible display time must be hidden.
questions.explanation: Versioning is safer when ordering carries business meaning.

questions.question: Why should TTL-based deletion be treated carefully in replicated systems?
options: Because skew or lag can make one replica delete data before others or before consumers have safely observed the change.
Because tombstones are never used in distributed stores.
Because deletion can only happen on leaders.
Because time-based expiry removes the need for replay.
questions.explanation: Deletion timing interacts with lag, repair, and ordering.

questions.question: What makes a time-assumptions answer sound senior?
options: It separates display time from correctness ordering, adds fencing or buffers where needed, and makes skew observable.
It assumes NTP makes distributed timing exact enough for every invariant.
It replaces every version with a timestamp.
It avoids discussing leases or expiry risk.
questions.explanation: Senior answers know where clocks help and where they mislead.
